title: Optimal Compute Allocation
concept: For a fixed training compute budget, Chinchilla scaling dictates the optimal balance of parameter size and training tokens to minimize final loss.

exercises.prompt: What are the two main ingredients we must balance when training an AI model within a budget- resources we must balance when training an AI model?

options.text: The weight of the computer and the speed of the internet
The number of programmers and the office size

exercises.explain: To train an AI, we need both the brain structure (parameters) and the learning material (training data/tokens). Compute budget is the energy and hardware used to combine them.

cards.title: Bigger Brain or More Books?
cards.body: If you had a fixed budget to build the ultimate AI, would you spend it on a bigger brain or more books? It turns out, doing too much of either is a waste. Let's learn how to balance them perfectly!

cards.title: Optimal Compute Allocation
cards.body: Optimal Compute Allocation (often called Chinchilla Scaling) is the formula for finding the perfect balance between the size of an AI model (its parameters) and the amount of text it reads (training tokens) for a set budget, so the AI learns as much as possible.

cards.title: The Cookie Budget
cards.body: Imagine you have $100 to spend on baking cookies for a party. You need to buy cookie dough (model parameters) and hire bakers (training tokens/data). If you spend all $100 on cookie dough but have no bakers to bake it, you get no cookies. If you hire 100 bakers but only buy one scoop of dough, they will sit idle. To get the most cookies, you need to split your budget perfectly between dough and bakers!

cards.title: The Chinchilla Discovery
cards.body: In 2022, researchers realized older models like GPT-3 had massive brain sizes (parameters) but weren't fed enough books (tokens). By training a smaller model named Chinchilla on much more data, they created a smarter AI using the exact same amount of computer power!

cards.title: Myth: Bigger is Always Better
cards.body: Many people believe that to make a smarter AI, you just need to make the model bigger. In reality, a giant model trained on too little data is actually weaker and more expensive than a smaller model trained on a massive amount of data.

cards.title: Balancing Scale
cards.body: See how changing the balance affects how smart the AI gets for the same compute cost.

left.text: Giant model trained on a small library of books. It is slow, expensive, and under-trained.

right.text: Medium-sized model trained on a massive library. It learns way more for the exact same budget.

exercises.prompt: According to the Chinchilla paper, why were older large language models like GPT-3 considered inefficient?

options.text: They were too large for the amount of data they were trained on.
They were trained for too many years.
They used too much data for their small size.
They were too cheap to build.

exercises.explain: Chinchilla scaling showed that earlier models were 'under-trained' because they had too many parameters relative to the small amount of data they were trained on.

exercises.prompt: True or False: If you double your compute budget, you should spend all of it on making the model larger while keeping the data size the same.
exercises.explain: If you double your computer power budget, you should split it to make the model larger and feed it more data, not just one or the other.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank to complete the scaling rule:
exercises.sentence: Optimal compute allocation means scaling model size and training data in equal proportions.
exercises.explain: To get the best results, you must scale both parameters (model size) and tokens (data) in equal proportions as your compute budget grows.

exercises.prompt: Which of these statements accurately describes optimal compute allocation?

options.text: "Optimal compute allocation means balancing parameters and training tokens equally to get the lowest possible loss for our budget."
"To make a model smarter, always spend 100% of your compute budget on increasing the parameter count, ignoring data."
"Parameters do not matter at all; compute budget should only be spent on collecting more training tokens."

exercises.explain: Option B is generated by an AI that doesn't understand the concept, incorrectly claiming that parameters are the only thing that matters. Option C is also wrong because it ignores model size. Option A correctly captures the balanced nature of Chinchilla scaling.

exercises.prompt: You are given a budget to build the most capable AI model possible. According to Chinchilla scaling laws, what is the best strategy to allocate your budget?

options.text: Make the model as massive as possible and train it on a tiny dataset.
Keep the model tiny and train it on an infinite amount of data.
Increase both the model size and the training data size in equal proportion.
Only buy faster hardware and keep the model and data sizes the same.

exercises.explain: Chinchilla scaling rules state that to get the most out of your compute budget, you should scale the size of your model (parameters) and the amount of training data (tokens) in equal proportion. doubling one without the other is highly inefficient!
